title: Can Ineos Grenadiers return to superteam status?
content:
Cyclist
Can Ineos Grenadiers return to superteam status?

Once upon a time, Grand Tour wins came in abundance to the squad formerly known as Team Sky. By the end of 2018, they had amassed six Tour de France wins, one Vuelta a España title and one Giro d’Italia, and that’s before getting onto all the other races.

Yet fast forward six years and Ineos Grenadiers looked a shadow of their former selves. No direction, ten wins outside of national championships, key riders leaving and not much hope for the future. It was a team that appeared to be just falling apart at the seams.

Things look different in 2026. The squad appears rejuvenated thanks to big signings including Oscar Onley and Kévin Vauquelin, and now it looks like a huge influx of cash is coming their way. Can they really return to the glory days?

Transfer market triumph

A whole host of riders retired in 2025, with the old guard of Jonathan Castroviejo, Omar Fraile, Salvatore Puccio calling it a day alongside momentary signing Caleb Ewan and the final memory of the Team Sky Tour de France heyday Geraint Thomas. Big shoes to fill, but a big opportunity to reset.

The obvious replacement for Thomas required a hefty sum, but parting ways with a few million euros for Oscar Onley seemed like a necessity. The Scotsman arrived off the back of a breakout season that included a mammoth fourth place at the Tour aged just 22. And that was without the kind of team support GC-focused squads like UAE Team Emirates XRG or Visma-Lease a Bike enjoy. Even in the shorter stage races he poses a threat, a third place at the Tour de Suisse and fifth place at the UAE Tour in his legs last year and a fourth place at the Volta ao Algarve for his first race in Ineos Grenadiers orange and white.

Frenchman Kévin Vauquelin has also been enjoying his start to 2026 safe from the sudden collapse of former team Arkéa-B&B Hotels. The two newbies are closely matched, Vauquelin finishing second at the Tour de Suisse and seventh at the Tour last season, and fifth place at Algarve this year. He is currently fourth overall in a strange edition of Paris-Nice, and was ahead of Onley before the Scot abandoned.

The pair will be a key part of future success for Ineos Grenadiers, especially in the Grand Tours. Ineos Grenadiers have been reliant on 2019 Tour and 2020 Giro winner Egan Bernal and 25-year-old Carlos Rodríguez for GC hopes. Bernal is not the same rider he once was having survived a career-derailing crash in 2022, but he has shown signs of real improvement, finishing seventh at least year’s Giro and winning a stage at the Vuelta. Rodríguez’s best result at the Tour was fifth in 2023, and has struggled to improve from there. They also have inconsistent Dutchman Thymen Arensman, who has double figures of Grand Tour participations in his 26-year-legs including several top tens and two very impressive stage wins at the Tour last year, but has been struggling to put together complete GC campaigns.

Whether the signing of sprinter Sam Welsford pays off is yet to be seen. The Australian has a win at the Tour Down Under in the bag and offers some more flexibility in an area they have been lacking in for quite some time, but a lot of focus has to go into obtaining and maintaining a solid leadout train. As GC hopes are now bolstered, you have to wonder whether this energy could be better served for that purpose like the Team Sky of old, especially given Welsford is a notch below the peloton’s top sprinters. However, the team bosses, including newly retired Geraint Thomas, are placing a high value on all kinds of victories, which is only a positive.

Thomas is part of a much-refreshed staff, with Zak Dempster and Oli Cookson leaving for Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe and new DSs including Daryl Impey and Elia Viviani brought on board, that could be the secret to putting the past few years of disappointment behind them.

A new title sponsor

This week several reports have indicated that Danish software consultancy Netcompany is set to make a big investment in the team. This is going to usher in a new era under a new name and new look, with Netcompany’s title sponsorship deal meaning they will replace Ineos in the team name. La Gazzetta dello Sport reported it was a five-year deal worth €100 million that came at just the right time, as other squads looking for further investment to compete with the likes of UAE Team Emirates, including Visma-Lease a Bike.

Ineos will still own the team, so Jim Ratfliffe isn’t fully out, and Dave Brailsford will be sticking around too, but from this year’s Tour de France the team will look and very very different.

Following a strong start to the season with stage victories at the Tour Down Under, Valenciana, Tirreno-Adriatico and Paris-Nice; second, third and seventh overall at the Tour de la Provence through Carlos Rodríguez, Brandon Rivera and Andrew August; fourth, fifth and ninth at Algarve through Onley, Vauquelin and Arensman, they’re getting numbers up there. Now it’s time to close the gap to the top step.

What does the future look like for Ineos Grenadiers?

A pathway for the future

Ineos Grenadiers were the last of the big WorldTour teams to develop a pathway or development team for younger riders. They did work on launching their own internal ‘Ascent’ programme that identified the likes of Josh Tarling, AJ August and Michael Leonard, but things took quite some time to produce something concrete.

In 2025 they partnered with German Continental outfit Lotto Kern-Haus PSD Bank, British junior cycling team Cams-Majaco and North America’s U19 outfit Hot Tubes Development Cycling Team, and they recently announced a new partnership with Italian junior programme Team Nordest Petrucci Assali Stefen too.

They have also launched the Ineos Grenadiers Racing Academy for 2026. Dubbed the cornerstone of the Grenadiers’ performance pathway that incorporates a UCI Continental team and elite young rider development programmes, there are 12 riders ranging from 18 to 23 years old currently signed up, and they just got their first win on the board too with Cameron Rogers winning the prologue at the Tour of Rhodes.

With the sport’s superstars breaking through so young, this was an essential step for the team to start to level the playing field with the other top WorldTour teams.

Superteam status still pending…

What defines a superteam? Big budgets, big names and big success. Of the current WorldTour crop, UAE Team Emirates XRG sit right at the top, thanks mostly to Tadej Pogačar, but their record haul of wins in 2025 show that it’s more than just a one-man show.

Cycling is, funnily enough, cyclical by nature, with periods of dominance coming and going. It’s unlikely Ineos/Netcompany will suddenly find themselves on the top step of the Tour de France anytime soon – though they have been linked with French supertalent Paul Seixas – but the signs are there that they are building something once again.

author: Robyn Davidson | Editorial Assistant
